name: Anal fissure
presentation: Severe anal pain during and after defecation — often described as 'passing broken glass'
Bright red rectal bleeding — streaking on stool or toilet paper
Acute: <6 weeks; Chronic: >6 weeks (this distinction drives management)
Risk factors: constipation, IBD (Crohn's), STIs (HIV, syphilis, herpes)
signs: Posterior midline tear on inspection — ~90% occur at posterior midline
Sentinel skin tag at lower end of fissure (chronic fissures)
Lateral or multiple fissures: consider Crohn's disease, syphilis, HIV

bedside: Inspection only: PR examination avoided acutely — too painful; no additional diagnostic benefit over inspection
Proctoscopy/sigmoidoscopy: deferred until after treatment if IBD or malignancy suspected
bloods: FBC/CRP: if IBD suspected (chronic or atypical fissures)
STI screen: if non-midline or multiple fissures — HIV, syphilis, HSV
imaging: MRI pelvis or colonoscopy: only if Crohn's disease suspected or red flag features suggesting malignancy

mx: Acute (<6 weeks): stool softening — high fibre diet, increased fluids; bulk-forming laxatives first-line (lactulose if not tolerated); topical anaesthetics; lubricants (petroleum jelly) before defecation

Chronic (>6 weeks): continue above PLUS topical glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) 0.4% — first-line pharmacological treatment; relaxes internal anal sphincter, reduces pressure, promotes blood flow and healing
Topical diltiazem: second-line if GTN not tolerated (headache); oral calcium channel blockers also second-line
If topical GTN fails after 8 weeks: refer to secondary care
Lateral internal sphincterotomy: gold standard surgical treatment for chronic fissure not responding to medical therapy
Botulinum toxin injection: used if sphincterotomy fails or is contraindicated
traps: Acute fissure <6 weeks = laxatives + topical anaesthetic only; topical GTN is for CHRONIC fissures (>6 weeks) — do not jump to GTN for a new fissure
Topical GTN, not buccal GTN — topical application delivers local effect; buccal gives excess systemic absorption with worse headache and no extra benefit
Oral/topical calcium channel blockers (diltiazem) = second-line after GTN failure, not first-line
Topical steroids: no role — do not reduce sphincter tone; long-term use causes skin thinning
Lateral or multiple fissures ≠ idiopathic — think Crohn's, HIV, syphilis, herpes; always check location
Sphincterotomy risk: faecal incontinence (internal sphincter division) — discuss before surgery; botulinum toxin is reversible alternative
